tossups.question: Mark Meadow argues that this painting’s use of thematic clusters was informed by the treatise Copia: Foundations of the Abundant Style. This painting [emphasize] does not include written words, a departure from a similar print by Frans Hogenberg. Near the center of this painting, a stork dips its head into a pitcher while sitting across a table from a fox. In this painting, a woman puts a blue cloak on her husband, an act that gives this painting its alternate name. In the lower right of this painting, a man fails to stretch his arms to reach two loaves of bread, an image intended to evoke the idea of struggling to live within a budget. This painting uses a high vantage point to depict a number of street scenes, similar to its artist’s depiction of The Fight Between Carnival and Lent. For 10 points, name this painting by Pieter Bruegel (“BROY-gull” or “BRUH-hull”) the Elder, in which peasants act out literal depictions of Dutch idioms.
tossups.answer: Netherlandish Proverbs [or Nederlandse Spreekwoorden; accept The Blue Cloak until read] (Copia is a treatise by Erasmus.)

packet.name: L (Delaware, UCSD A, and Amherst A)

tossups.answer_sanitized: Netherlandish Proverbs [or Nederlandse Spreekwoorden; accept The Blue Cloak until read] (Copia is a treatise by Erasmus.)

tossups.question: In this painting’s centre a fox and a stork sit at a table to dine together, while nearby a man defecates out of a window into a river. Yellow tarts indicating wealth can be seen on the roof of a house in this painting originally named for its depiction of a woman putting a blue cloak on her husband, signifying that he is being cuckolded. Elsewhere, one man bangs his head against a brick wall and another tries to swim against the tide. In all, some 112 Dutch sayings are represented in, for 10 points, which painting by Pieter Bruegel the Elder?
tossups.answer: Netherlandish Proverbs (or Flemish Proverbs; allow The Topsy Turvy World or The Folly of the World or The Blue Cloak before mention)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Netherlandish Proverbs (or Flemish Proverbs; allow The Topsy Turvy World or The Folly of the World or The Blue Cloak before mention)
tossups.question_sanitized: In this painting's centre a fox and a stork sit at a table to dine together, while nearby a man defecates out of a window into a river. Elsewhere, one man bangs his head against a brick wall and another tries to swim against the tide. In all, some 112 Dutch sayings are represented in, for 10 points, which painting by Pieter Bruegel the Elder?

tossups.question: In the background of this painting, a man watches as two bears dance with their backs to each other. In its right foreground, a man wearing a red tunic and a feathered cap seemingly controls a globe floating above his left hand. A woman ties the devil to a pillow in the bottom left of this painting, below a house that takes up the majority of its left half, from which a man urinates onto a sign. Sometimes called The Blue Cloak, it depicts two men holding each other by their noses, representing mutual trickery, over a stack of cards falling to the ground, representing the element of chance in man's fate. For 10 points, name this painting by Peter Bruegel the Elder, a literal depiction of idioms.
tossups.answer: Netherlandish Proverbs [or Flemish Proverbs or The Topsy Turvy World, accept The Blue Cloak before mention]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Netherlandish Proverbs [or Flemish Proverbs or The Topsy Turvy World, accept The Blue Cloak before mention]

bonuses.leadin: An artwork titled for these things painted on twelve small wooden plates shows a man urinating on an image of a crescent-shaped moon. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: An artwork titled for these things painted on twelve small wooden plates shows a man urinating on an image of a crescent-shaped moon. For 10 points each:
parts: Name these things that title a painting inspired by Frans Hogenberg’s The Blue Cloak that depicts a number of tarts left out on a rooftop above a man biting a pillar.
Netherlandish Proverbs was painted by this artist. He depicted villagers in the distance playing ice-stock and hockey in his landscape Hunters in the Snow.

Bruegel’s drawing of the proverb Big Fish Eat Little Fish was published by the “House of the Four Winds” company founded by this printmaker, who initially credited that drawing to Hieronymus Bosch instead to popularize the work.
parts_sanitized: Name these things that title a painting inspired by Frans Hogenberg's The Blue Cloak that depicts a number of tarts left out on a rooftop above a man biting a pillar.
Netherlandish Proverbs was painted by this artist. He depicted villagers in the distance playing ice-stock and hockey in his landscape Hunters in the Snow.

Bruegel's drawing of the proverb Big Fish Eat Little Fish was published by the "House of the Four Winds" company founded by this printmaker, who initially credited that drawing to Hieronymus Bosch instead to popularize the work.
answers_sanitized: proverbs [or spreekwoorden; accept idioms; accept sayings; accept Netherlandish Proverbs or Dutch Proverbs]
Pieter Bruegel the Elder [prompt on Pieter Bruegel]
Hieronymus Cock [or Hieronymus Wellens de Cock]

bonuses.leadin: In this painting, a man sitting on a window sill appears about to defecate on a globe with its cross pointing downwards. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this painting also depicting such tropes as a man knocking his head against a wall and a big fish eating a little fish.
Netherlandish Proverbs was painted by this Flemish artist of Landscape with the Fall of Icarus and The Triumph of Death.
This Bruegel painting shows the influence of Hieronymus Bosch, as the retreating title characters are in the middle of transforming into butterflies, fish, gnomes, and other bizarre creatures.
answers_sanitized: The Fall of the Rebel Angels
